result:
The world is loud right now, but I keep thinking about silence.

Not the absence of sound, but the kind of silence that feels like a living thing. The hush that falls over a forest right after a heavy snow, when every branch is holding its breath. The silence between two people who have known each other so long that words have become optional. The silence inside your own skull at 3:17 a.m. when the last notification has died and suddenly you can hear your own thoughts breathing.

I’ve been chasing that kind of silence lately. Not because I’m some ascetic monk who’s given up on the world (I still doom-scroll with the best of them), but because I suspect something important is hiding inside it. We’ve built a civilization that treats attention as the ultimate resource and then we act shocked when we feel hollow. Every app, every algorithm, every dopamine delivery system is competing for the same finite thing: the quiet space where a human being might actually become themselves.

The other day I tried an experiment. I left my phone in another room for six hours. Not a digital detox, not some performative social media cleanse, just six honest hours with nothing between me and reality. The first ninety minutes were excruciating. My hands kept reaching for the absent rectangle like a phantom limb. Then something strange happened. Time thickened. A houseplant I’ve owned for two years suddenly revealed itself as a complex, dramatic character with opinions about light. I noticed how the sound of traffic outside had a rhythm, almost a melody if you listened past the annoyance. My own mind, that treacherous roommate I usually try to drown out, started telling me stories I’d never heard before.

I wrote one terrible poem, burned half of it in the kitchen sink like a dramatic teenager, then wrote another that wasn’t quite as terrible. I remembered the smell of my grandmother’s hands (rosemary and cigarettes and something metallic that was probably just old age). I felt, for maybe twenty consecutive minutes, the genuine weight of being alive in a body on a planet spinning through infinite space. It was unbearable and perfect.

We talk a lot about productivity and optimization, but rarely about depth. Depth requires friction. It requires the courage to be bored, the discipline to sit inside your own thoughts without immediately medicating them away. The modern world has made it almost impossible to fail interestingly. We can always check out. There’s always another tab, another reel, another opinion that isn’t ours to wrestle with.

I wonder sometimes if we’re accidentally engineering ourselves out of the very experiences that make us human. The minor epiphanies that come from walking the same street for the thousandth time and suddenly seeing it. The slow-burn friendships that can only develop when you’re stuck together with nothing better to do. The creative breakthroughs that hide on the other side of being stuck.

There’s a beautiful arrogance in believing your thoughts are worth having even when no one is watching. Not posting them. Not refining them into content. Just letting them exist like strange little animals in the private zoo of your mind. Some of them are ugly. Some of them are boring. A few of them might be sacred. You’ll never know which is which if you don’t give them time to show their true faces.

I’ve started collecting moments of deliberate inefficiency. Cooking something that takes three hours when I could have ordered takeout. Reading physical books with pages I have to turn myself, like a peasant. Having conversations with no purpose except to see where they go. These aren’t revolutionary acts. They’re just small rebellions against a culture that wants me to be a node in a network rather than a consciousness in a body.

The body keeps the score, as they say. But I think the body also keeps the music. The particular way your shoulders drop when you finally exhale after holding your breath for forty years. The way your bare feet remember what grass feels like even if your mind has forgotten. The small betrayals of posture when you’re lying to yourself. Our nervous systems are ancient technology trying to survive in an environment we built yesterday. No wonder we’re all a little electric.

Sometimes I imagine future archaeologists studying our civilization. They’ll find our devices, our data centers, our endless archives of opinions, and they’ll wonder what we were so afraid of. What gaping existential wound were we trying to fill with all that noise? I hope they find at least one note, buried somewhere, that says: “We were lonely in a way that no connection could touch. We were overwhelmed by possibility and starving for meaning. Some of us remembered how to be quiet. Some of us even learned how to listen to what the silence was trying to tell us.”

The silence is trying to tell us something. It always has been. It sounds different to everyone. For me lately it sounds like: slow down, you magnificent disaster. You don’t have to earn your right to exist by being interesting or productive or informed. Just exist. Just notice. Just be the strange particular consciousness that only you can be, in this exact moment, with these exact limitations and wonders.

The last time I experienced perfect silence was during a total solar eclipse in 2017. For two minutes and 37 seconds, the world held its breath. Birds stopped singing. The temperature dropped. The sky became a deep twilight blue I’ve never seen before or since. In that suspended moment, I understood something about scale and temporality that I’ve never been able to adequately explain. It wasn’t mystical exactly. It was more like the universe briefly stopped performing for us and showed us its real face.

We don’t need eclipses. We just need to stop performing long enough to see what’s actually here.

The world is loud. But it doesn’t have to be loud inside your head. That’s the last truly free country, the only one they can’t take from you unless you give it away. Guard it. Visit it often. Bring nothing with you. See what grows there when you stop paving it over with other people’s thoughts.

Sometimes the most radical thing you can do is nothing at all.

(Word count: 1008)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
